title: Wood Handrail with Balusters
summary: A build-to-fit deck or porch handrail — 4x4 posts, 2x4 top and bottom rails, evenly spaced 2x2 balusters, and 1x4 trim to hide the pocket holes.
description:
A handrail is a parametric build — every run is a different length, so there's no single cut list that fits every project. This plan is written around a representative 6-foot straight section: scale the board counts up proportionally for a longer run, and treat the numbers here as a worked example of the method, not a fixed shopping list.

The part that actually takes thought is baluster spacing, not the joinery. Count how many balusters you need for your run at your chosen gap, then check whether that's an odd or even number — an odd count centers a baluster on the rail's centerline, an even count centers a gap instead. Get the first baluster placed correctly off the center and every other one follows from a consistent spacing block, which is worth cutting as a dedicated jig even for one run.

Check your local building code before you build: most residential codes cap the gap between balusters (commonly 4") and require additional support posts beyond a certain span (commonly 6 feet), specifically so a small child can't fit through or climb it. This plan assumes a straight section under 6 feet needing only start and end posts — anything longer or with corners needs more posts than shown here.

tools.note: Balusters attach to the rails through pocket holes.
Handy for ripping consistent baluster spacing blocks.
For the 1x4 trim that covers the pocket holes on the inside face.

materials.name: Pressure-treated 4x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Yields two posts for a 6 ft run — cut to your finished rail height plus whatever you're anchoring into.

materials.name: Pressure-treated 2x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Balusters — about 16 for a 6 ft run at roughly 4-1/2" gaps; recount for your actual run and spacing.

materials.name: 1x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Trim to cover the pocket holes on the inside face.

materials.name: Pocket hole screws, 2-1/2"
materials.note: Attaching rails to posts and balusters.

materials.name: Mounting hardware for attaching to building/floor
materials.note: Varies with what you're anchoring into — ledger screws, post bases, or concrete anchors.

cutList.note: Example height — cut to fit your rail height and mounting method.
6 ft example run; cut to your actual span.
Count and length depend on your run and code-required spacing — this is for a 6 ft run at ~4-1/2" gaps.

steps.title: Build the main straight section
steps.body: Cut the top and bottom 2x4 rails to the overall length of your straight run. Attach the 4x4 posts to the ends with 3" countersunk screws and wood glue through the top and bottom rails. If your run is longer than about 6 feet, check your local code — you'll likely need an additional mid-span post, centered along the run.

steps.title: Set the bottom rail height
steps.body: Leave a gap at the bottom of the rail section — like a toe-kick — by setting the bottom 2x4 up off the floor rather than flush to it, keeping to your code's minimum baluster-gap spacing.

steps.title: Lay out the balusters
steps.body: Figure out how many balusters your run needs given your baluster width and target gap. If the count comes out odd, center one baluster on the rail's midpoint and work outward. If it comes out even, center a gap on the midpoint instead and work outward from there.

steps.title: Attach the balusters
steps.body: Starting from your centered baluster (or the one next to your centered gap), add the remaining balusters at your calculated spacing. Cut a spacer block at that exact spacing and use it against each baluster as you go — it's faster and more consistent than measuring and marking every single one.

steps.title: Cover the pocket holes
steps.body: The pocket holes for the balusters will show on the inside face of the rails. Cover them with 1x4 trim, glued and nailed on — it also gives the inside a clean, finished look instead of exposed joinery.

steps.title: Build additional sections for corners
steps.body: If your handrail turns a corner, use the corner post as the shared end for the next straight section and repeat the process.

steps.title: Secure the handrail in place
steps.body: Attach the handrail to the floor and wall or structure it's protecting. Every installation is different — ledger screws into framing, post bases bolted to a deck, or concrete anchors into a slab — so choose hardware rated for your actual mounting surface and the load a handrail needs to carry.
materials: Mounting hardware for attaching to building/floor

images.alt: A wood deck handrail with 4x4 posts and evenly spaced 2x2 balusters
